name: Toyota Human Support Robot (HSR)
short_description: A compact, omnidirectional mobile manipulator with a folding arm, designed to assist elderly and disabled people with everyday physical tasks at home.
long_description: The HSR is a lightweight, cylindrical wheeled robot with a single folding arm, an actuated head carrying RGB-D and stereo cameras, and a display for telepresence, built to fetch objects, pick items off the floor, and help with daily living. Since 2015 Toyota has distributed it to universities and labs worldwide as a shared open research platform running ROS, making it one of the most common testbeds for in-home service robotics and human-robot interaction. It matters for physical AI in daily life because it grounds embodied assistance research in a real, repeatable domestic robot rather than a simulation, letting many groups benchmark navigation, manipulation, and care tasks on the same hardware.

images.caption: Toyota Human Support Robot (HSR), showing its camera head, telepresence display, and folding gripper arm.
images.alt_text: A white cylindrical Toyota Human Support Robot with a sensor-laden head, a small display screen, and a black folding gripper arm reaching forward.

sources.title: Toyota Shifts Home Helper Robot R&D into High Gear with New Developer Community and Upgraded Prototype
sources.access_notes: Official Toyota global newsroom announcement of the HSR developer community and platform.

sources.title: Toyota Human Support Robot: What is it and how can it be used?
sources.access_notes: Describes HSR capabilities, remote operation, and history since 2012.

interactions.title: Pick up what you dropped
interactions.description: Drop a pen, a remote, or a phone on the floor and the HSR rolls over, lowers its single arm, and hands it back up to you — the task it was built around for people who can't easily bend down.

interactions.title: A relative drives it into your living room
interactions.description: A distant family member or caregiver takes remote control of the robot, their face and voice on its display, and steers it around your home to check on you and fetch things in real time.

interactions.title: A grip that yields instead of breaking
interactions.description: When the arm presses a cup onto the floor or works a door handle, backdrivable QDD-actuated joints let it soak up the unexpected force on contact rather than jamming or snapping — a softer touch for cluttered, imprecise homes.
interactions.source_label: Toyota — Changing the Future Through Gentle Touch

interactions.title: Draw what you want done
interactions.description: Instead of joysticking the arm, an operator sketches on the camera feed: a line on the floor for where to drive, a C-shape around an object to grasp it. The robot reads the depth behind the doodle and turns it into motion.
interactions.source_label: Toyota — Controlling Robots Remotely with Sketches

interactions.title: The same body, contested by labs worldwide
interactions.description: Chosen as the standard robot for RoboCup@Home's Domestic Standard Platform League, identical HSRs face off at international tournaments running each team's own code for fetching, carrying, and finding people around a mock house.

interactions.title: Raising your hand from a hospital bed
interactions.description: Homebound students who normally attend class through wheeled telepresence robots piloted the HSR in a study and used its arm to literally raise a hand and pass plastic fruit to classmates — small physical acts their flat-screen-on-wheels robots couldn't do.
interactions.source_label: Evaluation of the HSR for Social Interaction and Learning (PMC)

interactions.title: Morning routine on a map
interactions.description: Because it navigates autonomously between any two points on a stored floor map, the HSR could run a fixed daily loop — open the curtains, bring the pill organizer from the kitchen, then park itself by the door — triggered by a single voice command.

interactions.title: Fetch by name from the shelf
interactions.description: With its RGB-D head and arm that already reaches high shelves, the robot could let you say which book or mug you want and have it visually pick that one out of a row and bring it over, rather than only grasping whatever is pointed at.
